subject: Mathematics
q: The number of integers greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn></math> that can be formed, using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math>, without repetition is
question: The number of integers greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn></math> that can be formed, using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math>, without repetition is
solution:
4 digit and 5 digit numbers are possible.

4 digit numbers:

6 / 7 / 8 ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ 3 4 3 2           Total numbers possible = 3 . 4 . 3 . 2 = 72

5 digit numbers

↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ ↑ ¯ 5 4 3 2 1           Total numbers possible = 5 . 4 . 3 . 2 . 1 = 120

∴Total numbers=72+120=192

subject: Mathematics
q: Number greater than 1000 but less than 4000 is formed using the digits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (repetition allowed) is
question: Number greater than 1000 but less than 4000 is formed using the digits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (repetition allowed) is
solution: Required number of numbers $=3 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5=375$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways of distributing 8 identical balls in 3 distinct boxes so that none of the boxes is empty is
question: The number of ways of distributing 8 identical balls in 3 distinct boxes so that none of the boxes is empty is
solution:
Number of balls $=8$
number of boxes $=3$
Hence number of ways $={ }^7 C_2=21$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of 3-digit numbers, formed using the digits $2,3,4,5$ and 7 , when the repetition of digits is not allowed, and which are not divisible by 3 , is equal to__________
question: The number of 3-digit numbers, formed using the digits $2,3,4,5$ and 7 , when the repetition of digits is not allowed, and which are not divisible by 3 , is equal to__________
solution:
total number of three digit numbers not divisible by 3 will be formed by using the digits
$\begin{aligned}
& (4,5,7) \\
& (3,4,7) \\
& (2,5,7) \\
& (2,4,7) \\
& (2,4,5) \\
& (2,3,5) \\
& \text { number of ways }=6 \times 3 !=36
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: There are two urns. Urn A has 3 distinct red balls and urn B has 9 distinct blue balls. From each urn two balls are taken out at random and then transferred to the other. The number of ways in which this can be done is
question: There are two urns. Urn A has 3 distinct red balls and urn B has 9 distinct blue balls. From each urn two balls are taken out at random and then transferred to the other. The number of ways in which this can be done is
solution:
Total number of ways $={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2$
$$
=3 \times \frac{9 \times 8}{2}=3 \times 36=108
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of permutations, of the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> without repetition, which neither contain the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>153</mn></math> nor the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2467</mn></math>, is _______ .
question: The number of permutations, of the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> without repetition, which neither contain the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>153</mn></math> nor the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2467</mn></math>, is _______ .
solution:
Given,

The number 1,2,3,....,7

Now total numbers of 7 digit number will be 7! without repetition,

Now total numbers which contain string 154 will be 5!

Total numbers which contain string 2367 will be 4!

And number which contain both string 2367 & 154 will be 2

So, total number which does not contain string 154 or 2367 will be 7!-5!+4!-2

=5040-120+24-2

=5040-142=4898

Hence this is the required answer.

subject: Mathematics
q: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit numbers, whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, is ______.
question: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit numbers, whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, is ______.
solution:
Prime factorisation of 36=2×2×3×3

So, for number having G.C.D with 36 to be 2, the number should be odd multiple of 2 and does not have factor 3 and 9

⇒Odd multiple of 2 are

102,106,110,114…….998  225 numbers

⇒No. of multiples of 3 are

102,114,126…….990 75 numbers

Which also include multiple of 9

Hence, Required =225-75=150

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of numbers between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> (repetition of digits is not allowed) and are multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is
question: The number of numbers between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> (repetition of digits is not allowed) and are multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is
solution:
When a number is divisible by 3, then sum of digits of a number is also divisible by 3.

Number can be formed from (0,1,2,3) or (0,2,3,4).

Number of 4 digits number

=3×3×2×1+3×3×2×1

=36.

subject: Mathematics
q: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit numbers whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is _____.
question: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit numbers whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is _____.
solution:
Let N be the four digit number gcdN,18=3

Hence N is an odd integer which is divisible by 3 but not by 9.

4 digit odd multiples of 3

1005,1011,…,9999→1500

digit odd multiples of 9

1017,1035,…,9999→500

Hence number of such N=1500-500=1000

subject: Mathematics
q: If the letters of the word MATHS are permuted and all possible words so formed are arranged as in a dictionary with serial numbers, then the serial number of the word THAMS is
question: If the letters of the word MATHS are permuted and all possible words so formed are arranged as in a dictionary with serial numbers, then the serial number of the word THAMS is
solution:
The given word is MATHS

Arranging the letters alphabetically, we get

AHMST

When the word starts with any of the letters A/H/M/S, the number of possibilities=4!×4=96

Now when the word starts with TA, then the number of possibilities=3!=6

Now when the word starts with THAMS, then the number of possibilities =1

Rank =96+6+1=103

Hence, rank of the work THAMS is 103.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then number of non-empty subsets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> such that the product of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is even is :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then number of non-empty subsets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> such that the product of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is even is :
solution:
As we know that the product of numbers is even when at least one of the number must be even.

Hence total subsets of A in which product of numbers is even = Total subsets – total subsets in which all the elements are odd

=2100-250=250250-1.

subject: Mathematics
q: The largest $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$ such that $3^{\mathrm{n}}$ divides 50 ! is:
question: The largest $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$ such that $3^{\mathrm{n}}$ divides 50 ! is:
solution:
$2^\alpha \cdot 3^\beta \cdot 5^\gamma$
$\mathrm{B}=\left[\frac{50}{3}\right]+\left[\frac{50}{3^2}\right]+\left[\frac{50}{3^3}\right]+\left[\frac{50}{3^4}\right]$
$=16+5+1$
$=2$
Maximum value of $n$ is 22

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>-digit natural numbers, such that the product of their digits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math>, is
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>-digit natural numbers, such that the product of their digits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math>, is
solution:
Let the five digit number be abcde

∵   36=22×32

Case I: When exactly one digit is 1

So, number of permutations of 2,2,3,3,1 will be

 =5!2!2!=30

Case II: When exactly two digits are 1

So, number of permutations of 4,3,3,1,1,6,2,3,1,1 or 9,2,2,1,1 will be

=5!2!2!+5!2!+5!2!2!=120

Case III: When exactly three digits are 1

So, number of permutations of 4,9,1,1,1 or 6,6,1,1,1 will be

=5!3!+5!3!2!=30

Hence, the required numbers=30+120+30=180

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways, in which 16 oranges can be distributed to four children such that each child gets at least one orange, is
question: The number of ways, in which 16 oranges can be distributed to four children such that each child gets at least one orange, is
solution:
Distributing 16 identical oranges to 4 children with each getting at least 1.
Let $x_i \ge 1$ for $i = 1,2,3,4$ and $x_1+x_2+x_3+x_4 = 16$.
Substituting $y_i = x_i - 1 \ge 0$: $y_1+y_2+y_3+y_4 = 12$.
By stars and bars, number of ways $= \binom{12+3}{3} = \binom{15}{3} = \frac{15 \times 14 \times 13}{6} = 455$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>-</mo></math> digit numbers in which only and all the five digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> appears, is
question: Total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>-</mo></math> digit numbers in which only and all the five digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> appears, is
solution:
As we have to make a six-digit number by using all the digits 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, hence exactly one of the digits is repeating.

For the digit to repeat we have 5C1=5 choices.
And, six digits in which one is repeating can be arranged in 6!2! ways.
Hence, total such numbers =5×6!2!=526!.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let p be the number of all triangles that can be formed by joining the vertices of a regular polygon P of n sides and q be the number of all quadrilaterals that can be formed by joining the vertices of $P$. If $p+q=126$, then the eccentricity of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{n}=1$ is :
question: Let p be the number of all triangles that can be formed by joining the vertices of a regular polygon P of n sides and q be the number of all quadrilaterals that can be formed by joining the vertices of $P$. If $p+q=126$, then the eccentricity of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{n}=1$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Total trangles }=\Rightarrow={ }^{\mathrm{h}} \mathrm{C}_3 \\ & \text { Total auadrilaterals }={ }^{\mathrm{h}} \mathrm{C}_4=\mathrm{q} \\ & { }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_3+{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_4=126 \Rightarrow{ }^{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{C}_4=126 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}+1=9 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=8 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{16}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{\mathrm{n}}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{16}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{8}=1 \\ & \mathrm{e}=\sqrt{1-\frac{8}{16}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{16}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>11</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. The number of non-empty subsets of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> that have the sum of all elements a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, is _____ .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>11</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. The number of non-empty subsets of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> that have the sum of all elements a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, is _____ .
solution:
Given set is

S=1,2,3,5,7,10,11

Elements of the type 3k is 3.

Elements of the type 3k+1 are 1,7,9.

Elements of the type 3k+2 are 2,5,11

Subset containing one element S1=1

Subsets containing two elements S2 

=C13×C13=9

Subsets containing three elements S3

=C13×C13+1+1=11

Subsets containing four elements S4

=C33+C33+C23×C23=11

Subsets containing five elements S5

=C23×C23×1=9

Subsets containing six elements S6=1

Subsets containing seven elements S7=1

Required number

=1+9+11+11+9+1+1

=43

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways of selecting two numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is the remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>23</mn></math> is
question: The number of ways of selecting two numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is the remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>23</mn></math> is
solution:
Let a=2,4,6,...,100 and b=1,3,5,...,99

Let

a+b=23λ+2

If λ=1, then

a+b=25

So, 1,24,3,22,....,23,2

Total ordered pairs=12

If λ=2, then

a+b=48

No ordered pair possible.

If λ=3, then

a+b=71

So,

1,70, 3,68, 5,66,....,61,10, 63,8, 65,6, 67,4, 69,2

Total 35 ordered pairs.

If λ=5, then

a+b=117

17,100, 19,98, ..., 99,18

Total 42 ordered pairs.

If λ=7, then

a+b=163

So,

63,100, 65,63,.... 99,64

Total 19 pairs.

No further case possible.

So, required number is

=12+35+42+19=108

subject: Mathematics
q: In a group of 3 girls and 4 boys, there are two boys $B_1$ and $B_2$. The number of ways, in which these girls and boys can stand in a queue such that all the girls stand together, all the boys stand together, but $B_1$ and $B_2$ are not adjacent to each other, is :
question: In a group of 3 girls and 4 boys, there are two boys $B_1$ and $B_2$. The number of ways, in which these girls and boys can stand in a queue such that all the girls stand together, all the boys stand together, but $B_1$ and $B_2$ are not adjacent to each other, is :
solution:
Total - when $\mathrm{B}_1$ and $\mathrm{B}_2$ are together
$=2!(3!4!)-2!(3!(3!2!))=144$

subject: Mathematics
q: Assuming the balls to be identical except for difference in colours, the number of ways in which one or more balls can be selected from $10$ white, $9$ green and $7$ black balls is
question: Assuming the balls to be identical except for difference in colours, the number of ways in which one or more balls can be selected from $10$ white, $9$ green and $7$ black balls is
solution: Number of ways of selecting one or more balls from $10$ white, $9$ green, and $7$ black balls $=(10+1)(9+1)(7+1)-1=11 \times 10 \times 8-1=879.$

subject: Mathematics
q: A scientific committee is to be formed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> Indians and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> foreigners, which includes at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> Indians and double the number of foreigners as Indians. Then the number of ways, the committee can be formed, is:
question: A scientific committee is to be formed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> Indians and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> foreigners, which includes at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> Indians and double the number of foreigners as Indians. Then the number of ways, the committee can be formed, is:
solution:
Indians Foreigners Number of ways
2 4 C26×C48=1050
3 6 C36×C68=560
4 8 C46×C88=15

Total number of ways =1625

subject: Mathematics
q: The lines L1,L2,...,L20 are distinct. For n=1,2,3,...,10 all the lines L2n−1 are parallel to each other and all the lines L2n pass through a given point P. The maximum number of points of intersection of pairs of lines from the set L1,L2,...,L20 is equal to:
question: The lines L1,L2,...,L20 are distinct. For n=1,2,3,...,10 all the lines L2n−1 are parallel to each other and all the lines L2n pass through a given point P. The maximum number of points of intersection of pairs of lines from the set L1,L2,...,L20 is equal to:
solution:
Given:

Out of 20 numbered lines, even numbered lines are concurrent, odd number lines are parallel.

So, the maximum number of intersection points will be given by,

N=1→point of intersection when both lines are even+0→point of intersection when both lines are parallel+C110×C110→point of intersection when 1 odd and 1 even line meets

⇒N=1+0+100

⇒N=101

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of 4 -letter words, with or without meaning, which can be formed using the letters PQRPQRSTUVP, is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The number of 4 -letter words, with or without meaning, which can be formed using the letters PQRPQRSTUVP, is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Letters: P(3), Q(2), R(2), S(1), T(1), U(1), V(1) — 7 distinct letters.
All distinct: $\binom{7}{4} \times 4! = 840$.
One pair + 2 distinct: choose repeating letter from $\{P,Q,R\}$ (3 ways), choose 2 from remaining 6 ($\binom{6}{2}=15$), arrange $= 4!/2! = 12$. Total $= 3 \times 15 \times 12 = 540$.
Two pairs: choose 2 from $\{P,Q,R\}$ ($\binom{3}{2}=3$), arrange $= 4!/(2!2!) = 6$. Total $= 18$.
One triple + 1 distinct: only P can repeat 3 times, choose 1 from 6, arrange $= 4!/3! = 4$. Total $= 24$.
Answer $= 840 + 540 + 18 + 24 = 1422$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of three-digit even numbers, formed by the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> if the repetition of digits is not allowed, is______.
question: The number of three-digit even numbers, formed by the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> if the repetition of digits is not allowed, is______.
solution:
We have,

0,1,3,4,6,7

For three-digit even number, it must end with either 0, 4, 6

The number of three -digit even numbers ending with 0 is

=5×4=20

The number of three -digit even numbers ending with 4

=4×4=16 

The number of three -digit even numbers ending with 6

=4×4=16

 Total three digits even numbers =20+16+16=52

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of seven digit positive integers formed using the digits 1,2,3 and 4 only and sum of the digits equal to 12 is_______.
question: The number of seven digit positive integers formed using the digits 1,2,3 and 4 only and sum of the digits equal to 12 is_______.
solution:
The given numbers are 1,2,3,4 and sum of digits should be 12.

Case 1: 4 3 1 1 1 1 1 ,Here the sum of the digits is 12.

The number of ways arranging them is =7!5!=42.

Case 2: 4 2 2 1 1 1 1.

The number of ways arranging them is =7!2!4!=105

Case 3: 3 2 2 2 1 1 1.

The number of ways arranging them is =7!3!3!=140

Case 4: 3 3 2 1 1 1 1.

The number of ways arranging them is =7!2!4!=105

Case 5: 2 2 2 2 2 1 1

The number of ways arranging them is =7!5!2!=21

The total number of ways are =42+105+140+105+21

=413

Therefore, the required answer is 413.

subject: Mathematics
q: For $\mathrm{n} \geq 2$, let $S_n$ denote the set of all subsets of $\{1,2 \ldots . . ., n\}$ with no two consecutive numbers. For example $\{1,3,5\} \in \mathrm{S}_6$, but $\{1,2,4\} \notin \mathrm{S}_6$. Then $n\left(\mathrm{~S}_5\right)$ is equal to ________
question: For $\mathrm{n} \geq 2$, let $S_n$ denote the set of all subsets of $\{1,2 \ldots . . ., n\}$ with no two consecutive numbers. For example $\{1,3,5\} \in \mathrm{S}_6$, but $\{1,2,4\} \notin \mathrm{S}_6$. Then $n\left(\mathrm{~S}_5\right)$ is equal to ________
solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4,5 \ldots . . . \mathrm{n}\}$
No. of subsets having $r$ elements such that no two are consecutive is $={ }^{n-r+1} C_r$
for $\mathrm{n}=5$, no. of ways $={ }^{6 \cdot \mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$
Subsets having no element $=1$
Subsets having exactly 1 element $={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1=5$
Subsets having exactly 2 element $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2=6$
Subsets having exactly 3 element $={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_3=1$
$\Rightarrow 5+6+1+1=13$

subject: Mathematics
q: A number is called a palindrome if it reads the same backward as well as forward. For example <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>285582</mn></math> is a six digit palindrome. The number of six digit palindromes, which are divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is ________.
question: A number is called a palindrome if it reads the same backward as well as forward. For example <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>285582</mn></math> is a six digit palindrome. The number of six digit palindromes, which are divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is ________.
solution:
Palindrome Numbers only in this form will be divisible by 55.

Numbers can be  between 0-9

Number of ways of selecting value of a=P110

Similarly  number of ways of choosing value of b=P110  (As a and b can also be same)

Therefore, Total number of ways of getting palindrome numbers divisible by 55 is =1×10×10×1=100

subject: Mathematics
q: All the arrangements, with or without meaning, of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> are written excluding any word that has two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> appearing together. The arrangements are listed serially in the alphabetic order as in the English dictionary. Then the serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> in this list is _____ .
question: All the arrangements, with or without meaning, of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> are written excluding any word that has two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> appearing together. The arrangements are listed serially in the alphabetic order as in the English dictionary. Then the serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> in this list is _____ .
solution:
Number of such words without both R together

= total possible words - total possible words with both R together

Number of such words start with A=5!2!-4!=36

Number of such words start with E=5!2!-4!=36

Number of such words start with FAE=3!2!-2=1

Number of such words start with FAM=3!2!-2=1

Number of such words start with FARE=2!=2

Number of such words start with FARMER=1

Total =77

subject: Mathematics
q: If ${ }^n C_r$ denotes the number of combination of $n$ things taken $r$ at a time, then the expression ${ }^n C_{r+1}+{ }^n C_{r-1}+2 x^n C_r$ equals
question: If ${ }^n C_r$ denotes the number of combination of $n$ things taken $r$ at a time, then the expression ${ }^n C_{r+1}+{ }^n C_{r-1}+2 x^n C_r$ equals
solution:
After t; velocity $=\mathrm{f} \times \mathrm{t}$
$V_{B A}=\vec{f} t+(-\vec{u})=\sqrt{f^2 t^2+u^2-2 f u t \cos \alpha}$
For max. and min.
$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BA}}^2\right)=2 \mathrm{f}^2 \mathrm{t}-2 \mathrm{fu} \cos \alpha=0 \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{u} \cos \alpha}{\mathrm{f}}$
Therefore, total no. of values of $\mathrm{r}=33$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed by taking 4 letters at a time from the letters of the word 'SYLLABUS' such that two letters are distinct and two letters are alike, is
question: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed by taking 4 letters at a time from the letters of the word 'SYLLABUS' such that two letters are distinct and two letters are alike, is
solution:
From the given word, S-2,L-2,A,B,Y,U.

Number of alike letter pairs =2

Number of ways to select one pair=C12

Number of distinct letters=5

Number of ways to select two distinct letters=C25

Number of ways to arrange four letters if two are alike=4!2!

Required number of ways =C12C25·4!2!=2·10·242=240

subject: Mathematics
q: A committee of <math><mn>11</mn></math> member is to be formed from <math><mn>8</mn></math> males and <math><mn>5</mn></math> females. If <math><mi>m</mi></math> is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least <math><mn>6</mn></math> males and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> females, then:
question: A committee of <math><mn>11</mn></math> member is to be formed from <math><mn>8</mn></math> males and <math><mn>5</mn></math> females. If <math><mi>m</mi></math> is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least <math><mn>6</mn></math> males and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> females, then:
solution:
m=(6M, 5F)+(7M, 4F)+(8M, 3F)

=C6 8×C5 5+C7 8×C4 5+C8 8×C3 5

=28×1+8×5+1×10=78

n=(3F, 8M)+(4F, 7M)+(5F, 6M)

=C3 5×C8 8+C4 5×C7 8+C5 5×C6 8

=10×1+5×8+1×28=78

Hence, m=n=78.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math>-digit numbers which are multiples of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> and are formed using all the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> is _____.
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math>-digit numbers which are multiples of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> and are formed using all the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> is _____.
solution:
Digits given are 1,2,3,4,5,7,9

Now multiple of 11→ Difference of sum at even & odd place should be divisible by 11.

Let number of the form abcdefg

∴ a+c+e+g-b+d+f=11x

a+b+c+d+e+f=31

∴ either a+c+e+g=21 or 10

∴b+d+f=10 or 21

Case-1

a+c+e+g=21

b+d+f=10

b,d,f∈1,2,72,3,51,4,5

a,c,e,g∈1,4,7,9,3,4,5,9,2,3,7,9

∴ Total number in case-1 =3!×34!=432

Case-2

a+c+e+g=10

b+d+f=21

a,b,e,g∈1,2,3,4

b,d,f & 5,7,9

∴ Total number in case 2=3!×4!=144

∴  Total numbers =144+432=576

subject: Mathematics
q: All words, with or without meaning, are made using all the letters of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Y</mi></math>. These words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. The serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Y</mi></math> is
question: All words, with or without meaning, are made using all the letters of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Y</mi></math>. These words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. The serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Y</mi></math> is
solution:
The given word is MONDAY

Arranging the letters alphabetically, we get

ADMNOY

When the word starts with any of the letters A/D, the number of possibilities=5!×2=240

Now when the word starts with MA, then the number of possibilities=4!=24

Now when the word starts with MD, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with MN, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with MOA, then the number of possibilities=3!=6

Now when the word starts with MOD, then the number of possibilities =3!=6

Now when the word starts with MONA, then the number of possibilities =2!=2

Now when the word starts with MONDAY, then the number of possibilities=1

Rank =240+24×3+6×2+2+1=327

Hence, rank of the word MONDAY is 327.

SHORTCUT METHOD

Another method is a shortcut, first number the alphabet and then check how many number are there on right hand side less that number and then start number in factorial in decreasing order and then multiply and add them and in last add one to find rank, 

354216MONDAY2321005!4!3!2!1!0!

∴ Rank =(2×5!+3×4!+2×3!+1×2!)

=240+72+12+2+1=327

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider a class of <math><mn>5</mn></math> girls and <math><mn>7</mn></math> boys. The number of different teams consisting of <math><mn>2</mn></math> girls and <math><mn>3</mn></math> boys that can be formed from this class, if there are two specific boys <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> who refuse to be the members of the same team, is:
question: Consider a class of <math><mn>5</mn></math> girls and <math><mn>7</mn></math> boys. The number of different teams consisting of <math><mn>2</mn></math> girls and <math><mn>3</mn></math> boys that can be formed from this class, if there are two specific boys <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> who refuse to be the members of the same team, is:
solution:
7B5G→3B2G

Total number of ways of forming a team of 3 boys and 2 girls = 7C3× 5C2=7.6.51.2.3×5.41.2=350

Total number of ways of forming a team if two specific boys B1,B2 always join the team

= 5C1× 5C2=50

So, the total number of ways of forming a team so that two specific boys never come together

=350-50=300

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of integers, greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7000</mn></math> that can be formed, using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn></math> without repetition is
question: The number of integers, greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7000</mn></math> that can be formed, using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn></math> without repetition is
solution:
Case 1 

forming 4 digit number,

When 7 is at 1000's place

7 _ _ _,

So total number of ways will be 4×3×2=24 ways,

Case 2 

forming 4 digit number when 8 is at 1000's place 

8 _ _ _,

So total number of ways will be 4×3×2=24 ways,

Case 3 

forming 5 digit number,

So total number of ways will be 5!=120 ways,

Now adding all the cases we get, 120+24+24=168 ways.
